content.problemStatement: Create a program that takes a lowercase English alphabetic character as input and uses a **switch** statement to determine if it's a **vowel** or **consonant**.
constraints: Each character is a lowercase English letter (a‑z)
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 100), the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single lowercase English letter (a‑z).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a line containing "Vowel" if the character is a, e, i, o or u; otherwise output "Consonant".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Three test cases: 'a' and 'i' are vowels, so output Vowel; 'b' is a consonant, so output Consonant.
Single test case with 'z', which is not a vowel, therefore Consonant.
'e','u','o' are vowels and produce Vowel; 'c','d' are consonants and produce Consonant.

hidden.label: mixed case
hidden.input:
6
a
b
c
d
e
f
hidden.expectedOutput:
Vowel
Consonant
Consonant
Consonant
Vowel
Consonant

solution.approach: Read the integer T. For each of the T lines read the character. Using a switch (or a set) check whether the character is one of the five vowels {'a','e','i','o','u'}. If it matches, output "Vowel"; otherwise output "Consonant". The algorithm processes each test case independently, runs in linear time and uses constant extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – each test case is examined once.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few variables are stored regardless of T.

code.C:
#include <stdio.h>
int main(){int T; if(scanf("%d",&T)!=1)return 0; while(T--){ char ch; scanf(" %c",&ch); switch(ch){case 'a':case 'e':case 'i':case 'o':case 'u': printf("Vowel\n"); break; default: printf("Consonant\n");}} return 0; }
